lesson.title: File I/O and the io Package
lesson.summary: Read and write files, work with readers and writers.

sections.heading: Reading and writing files
sections.bodyHtml:
Use os.ReadFile and os.WriteFile for simple file operations (Go 1.16+):

data := []byte("hello world")
err := os.WriteFile("output.txt", data, 0644)

read, err := os.ReadFile("output.txt")
fmt.Println(string(read))  // hello world

For larger files, use os.Open + io.Copy or a bufio.Scanner to stream line-by-line:

f, err := os.Open("file.txt")
scanner := bufio.NewScanner(f)
for scanner.Scan() {
    fmt.Println(scanner.Text())
}

sections.heading: The io.Reader and io.Writer interfaces
sections.bodyHtml:
The io.Reader and io.Writer interfaces are the foundation of Go's I/O:

type Reader interface {
    Read(p []byte) (n int, err error)
}
type Writer interface {
    Write(p []byte) (n int, err error)
}

Many types satisfy these: files, network connections, strings, buffers. Use io.Copy to stream from any reader to any writer:

r := strings.NewReader("data")
w := os.Stdout
io.Copy(w, r)  // writes "data" to stdout

sections.heading: Working with the filesystem
sections.bodyHtml:
The os package provides filesystem operations:

os.Stat("file.txt")  // file info
os.Remove("file.txt") // delete file
os.Rename("old", "new") // rename or move

For virtual filesystems (useful in tests), use io/fs and testing/fstest.

sections.heading: Your turn
sections.bodyHtml:
Open practice/file-io/main.go.

Write a program that writes "Hello, file!" to hello.txt using os.WriteFile, then reads and prints it with os.ReadFile.

questions.prompt: Which function reads an entire file into memory (Go 1.16+)?

options.label: Copies the file from r to w
Reads from r and writes to w until EOF

exercises.title: Write and read a file
exercises.instructions: Write "Hello, file!" to hello.txt, then read and print it.
